questions.a: All possible configurations or solutions that an algorithm can explore.
Evaluates how good a solution is in optimization or evolutionary algorithms.
Chooses the best immediate option without considering long-term consequences.
Local: Best solution in a small region; Global: Best solution overall in the space.
Exploration: Trying new areas; Exploitation: Using known good areas to improve solutions.
Iterative search that moves to neighboring states with better fitness until a peak is reached.
Search algorithm that allows occasional worse moves to escape local optima, inspired by annealing in metallurgy.
Optimization method that updates parameters in the direction of the negative gradient to minimize loss.
Like GD but updates parameters using a random mini-batch, adding noise for better convergence.
Involves randomness; results may vary per run (e.g., SGD, evolutionary algorithms).
No randomness; same inputs always lead to same outputs (e.g., basic gradient descent).
Controls the step size during optimization; too high = overshoot, too low = slow learning.
The point where the optimization algorithm stabilizes or finds an optimal solution.
A group of candidate solutions in an evolutionary algorithm.
A single candidate solution in the population.
Genotype: Encoded representation of a solution; Phenotype: Actual behavior or output it produces.
A single value at a gene position in a chromosome.
Selects best individuals from randomly chosen groups.
Ranks individuals by fitness and selects probabilistically based on rank.
Probability of selection is proportional to fitness; like spinning a weighted wheel.
Reduces selection pressure by replacing similar individuals to preserve diversity.
Automatically carries the best individuals to the next generation.
Swaps tails of parents at one random crossover point.
Swaps segments of parents at multiple crossover points.
Each gene is randomly chosen from one of the parents with equal probability.
Blends parent values using weighted averages per allele.
Chooses each gene from one of the parents without averaging.
Partially Mapped Crossover - used for permutations; preserves absolute positions.
Creates offspring by alternating cycles from both parents to preserve position info.
Preserves relative order of genes from parents in offspring.
Preserves edge relationships between genes in parent solutions.
Flips bits (0 to 1 or vice versa) with a small probability.
Each gene is replaced by a random value uniformly sampled from its domain.
Degree to which fitter individuals are favored; too high can lead to premature convergence.
An EA where solutions evolve using selection, crossover, and mutation over generations.
Uses EA to evolve computer programs or symbolic expressions, often as trees.
Maintains variation in the population to avoid premature convergence.
Population is split into subpopulations (islands) that occasionally exchange individuals.
Divides population into species based on similarity to maintain diversity.
Reduces fitness of similar individuals to encourage diverse solutions.
When the population becomes too similar too early, potentially missing the global optimum.
Optimization involving more than one conflicting objective, e.g., speed vs. accuracy.
